title: Steak and Mushroom Pie
subtitle: Braised beef and chestnut mushrooms in a rich gravy under golden puff pastry
excerpt: Steak and mushroom pie uses the same method as the kidney version: a long, slow braise of braising steak, a deep gravy, and a puff pastry lid baked golden. The mushrooms provide body and earthiness in place of the kidney. The result is a reliable pub-style pie with no contentious ingredients.
sourceNotes: British pub cookery tradition. The steak and mushroom pie is a widely documented variant of the British beef pie, established as a standard menu item in British pubs and restaurants from the mid-20th century.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the cooked filling separately, cooled. Thaw overnight in the fridge, top with pastry, and bake at 200°C for 30 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the filling up to two days ahead and refrigerate. Top with pastry on the day and bake.

glossaryTerms.definition: A slow-cooking method where food is first browned in hot fat, then cooked in a small amount of liquid in a covered pot over low heat. The combination of dry and moist heat breaks down tough connective tissue, making cheaper cuts of meat tender and flavoursome.

content.text: Steak and mushroom pie follows the same logic as steak and kidney: braise the beef low and slow, build the gravy in the process, cool the filling, and finish under puff pastry. The mushrooms are not a garnish added at the end; they go in from the start and become part of the gravy. The long braise is what separates a good pie from a tough one.

Season the braising steak and toss in the {{plain-flour}} of plain flour. Heat a film of oil in a wide frying pan over a high heat and brown the beef in batches for 3 to 4 minutes per batch, turning once. Transfer to a casserole.

Reduce the heat to medium. Add the {{onion}} diced onion to the frying pan and cook for 4 minutes, scraping up the residue. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and tomato puree and stir for 1 minute. Tip everything into the casserole.
Add the {{chestnut-mushrooms}} of mushrooms to the casserole. Pour over the {{stock-beef}} of beef stock and add the Worcestershire sauce, thyme, and bay. Season well. Bring to a simmer, cover, and
over the lowest heat for 2 hours until the beef is completely tender. Remove the lid for the final 20 minutes to reduce the gravy. Leave to cool completely.

Heat the oven to 200°C. Spoon the cooled filling into a deep pie dish. Roll the puff pastry to fit the top with a little overhang. Lay it over the dish, press the edges to seal, and trim. Brush with beaten egg and cut two slits in the centre.
Bake for 25 to 30 minutes until the pastry is deep golden and the filling is bubbling at the edges. Rest for 10 minutes before serving.

items.symptom: Beef is still tough after 2 hours
items.cause: The heat was too high during braising, causing the meat to seize rather than relax.
items.fix: Braise at the lowest possible heat: the liquid should only just tremble, not simmer actively. Add a splash more stock and continue for another 30 minutes.

items.cause: The lid stayed on throughout, preventing any reduction.
items.fix: Remove the lid for the final 20 to 30 minutes of braising. If the filling is still loose when cold, transfer the liquid to a small pan, reduce by half, and return it to the filling.

items.symptom: Pastry is pale and soft on the underside
items.cause: Filling was too hot when the pastry went on, or the dish is too deep.
items.fix: Cool the filling completely before adding the pastry. Place the dish on a hot baking sheet in the oven to give the base of the filling more heat.

content.text: Steak and mushroom pie is the version of the British beef pie that requires no persuasion. Without kidney, it asks nothing contentious of the diner. The mushrooms do a different job to kidney: they absorb the beef gravy and provide earthy bulk rather than their own sharp note. The result is a gentler, rounder pie that has become the default on British pub menus precisely because it offends no one and satisfies everyone.
